text: The relationship between incomplete tasks and sleep is particularly illuminating. Sleep researchers have found that the hippocampus preferentially consolidates emotionally salient and unresolved experiences during slow-wave sleep — a process that may represent the brain's own attempt to 'close loops' overnight. This is one reason that intrusive thoughts about unfinished work spike at bedtime: the brain is preparing to process them and encounters them already active. Matthew Walker notes that REM sleep in particular serves a form of emotional processing, stripping the affective charge from difficult memories. An unresolved task carried to bed is not merely distraction — it is raw material the brain intends to work on.
question: According to the passage, why do intrusive thoughts about unfinished work spike at bedtime?

options.text: The prefrontal cortex becomes less active, reducing the ability to suppress thoughts
Fatigue amplifies the emotional weight attached to incomplete tasks
The brain is preparing to process these tasks and encounters them already active
Circadian rhythms shift attention from external tasks to internal states
